---
title: "Ch 3c — Trigonometric Equations"
subtitle: "When the equation is sin x = something — and there are infinitely many answers"
lesson-id: "cbse11.trig-equations"
tier: "cbse11"
ncert-class: 11
ncert-chapter: 3
ncert-chapter-title: "Trigonometric Functions"
board-exam: false
prerequisites: ["cbse11.trig-identities-compound-angles"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.relations-functions-types", "cbse12.continuity"]
applied-domains: ["research", "game-dev"]
estimated-minutes: 45
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Be honest before you begin.

1. Can you state $\sin\theta$ for the standard angles in any quadrant?
2. Do you remember $\sin$ has period $2\pi$ and $\tan$ has period $\pi$?
3. Are you fluent with compound-angle identities from Lesson 3b?
4. Can you sketch $y = \sin x$ on $[0, 2\pi]$?

Three yeses — read on. Otherwise revisit
**[Trigonometric Functions](../03a-trig-functions/index.qmd)** and
**[Compound Angles](../03b-trig-identities-compound-angles/index.qmd)**.
:::

## Touch

Draw the graph of $y = \sin x$ on the board, from $-2\pi$ to $2\pi$.
Now draw the horizontal line $y = 1/2$. **Count** the points where they
meet. There are four — and if you extended the picture left and right,
there would be infinitely many.

A trigonometric equation $\sin x = c$ doesn't have *one* answer. It has
a whole family — and the family has a clean pattern.

The widget below sweeps the value of $c$ and plots all solutions in
$[0, 2\pi)$.

```{ojs}
viewof cVal = Inputs.range([-1, 1], {value: 0.5, step: 0.01, label: "c (RHS of sin x = c)"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  // sin x = c on [0, 2pi)
  const c = cVal;
  if (Math.abs(c) > 1) return html`<div>No real solutions (|c| > 1).</div>`;
  const principal = Math.asin(c);   // in [-pi/2, pi/2]
  let s1, s2;
  if (principal >= 0) { s1 = principal; s2 = Math.PI - principal; }
  else { s1 = Math.PI - principal; s2 = 2 * Math.PI + principal; }
  const fmt = x => `${x.toFixed(4)} (${(x * 180 / Math.PI).toFixed(2)}°)`;
  return html`<div style="font-family:system-ui;line-height:1.7;">
    <div><b>sin x = ${c.toFixed(2)}</b></div>
    <div>Solutions in [0, 2π): ${fmt(s1)} and ${fmt(s2)}</div>
    <div style="margin-top:0.4em;color:#666;">
      All real solutions: x = (above) ± 2πk for any integer k.
      Or equivalently: x = nπ + (-1)ⁿ · principal, n ∈ ℤ.
    </div>
  </div>`;
}
```

Drag $c$. The two solutions move in lockstep. When $c = 1$ they merge;
when $|c| > 1$ they vanish.

## Wonder

Sit with these.

1. If $\sin x = 0$, where does $x$ live? List the answers between $-2\pi$
   and $2\pi$.
2. The equation $\sin x = 1/2$ has two solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$. The
   equation $\cos x = 1/2$ also has two. Are the *patterns* the same?
3. $\tan x = 1$ has only *one* solution in $[0, 2\pi)$ in some sense —
   but two in $[0, 2\pi)$. Why the discrepancy?
4. If $\sin x = \sin y$, does $x = y$ necessarily?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## What you might notice

(2) is the reason cosine and sine have *different* general-solution
formulas. (3) is why $\tan$ has period $\pi$, half of sine's period.
(4) explodes into the most common error in this chapter.
:::

## Discover

### Problem 1 — sin x = 0

List all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\sin x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin$ vanishes at $0, \pi, 2\pi, -\pi, -2\pi, \ldots$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin x = 0 \Leftrightarrow x = n\pi,\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
</details>

### Problem 2 — cos x = 0

List all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\cos x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\cos$ vanishes at $\pi/2, 3\pi/2, -\pi/2, \ldots$
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos x = 0 \Leftrightarrow x = (2n+1)\dfrac{\pi}{2},\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

Equivalently $x = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$.
</details>

### Problem 3 — tan x = 0

List all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ with $\tan x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan x = \sin x / \cos x = 0$ requires $\sin x = 0$ (and $\cos x \ne 0$).
So $x = n\pi$, same as $\sin x = 0$.
</details>

### Problem 4 — sin x = 1/2 in [0, 2π)

Find all solutions of $\sin x = 1/2$ on $[0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sine is positive in Q I and Q II.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = \pi/6$ (Q I) and $x = \pi - \pi/6 = 5\pi/6$ (Q II).
</details>

### Problem 5 — General solution of sin

Generalise: if $\sin x = \sin\alpha$, what are *all* solutions?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Both $\alpha$ and $\pi - \alpha$ have the same sine. Add multiples of
$2\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = n\pi + (-1)^n \alpha,\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

(For even $n$: $x = 2k\pi + \alpha$; for odd $n$: $x = (2k+1)\pi - \alpha$.
Both branches are captured by the $(-1)^n$ formula.)
</details>

### Problem 6 — General solutions for cos and tan

State the general solutions of $\cos x = \cos\alpha$ and
$\tan x = \tan\alpha$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Cosine: pairs $\alpha$ and $-\alpha$ have the same cosine. Period $2\pi$.

Tangent: just one shape per period $\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos x = \cos\alpha \Leftrightarrow x = 2n\pi \pm \alpha,\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

$\tan x = \tan\alpha \Leftrightarrow x = n\pi + \alpha,\ n \in \mathbb{Z}$.
</details>

### Problem 7 — Quadratic in sin

Solve $2\sin^2 x - 3\sin x + 1 = 0$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Let $u = \sin x$. Solve the quadratic in $u$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2u^2 - 3u + 1 = (2u - 1)(u - 1) = 0$, so $\sin x = 1/2$ or $\sin x = 1$.

$\sin x = 1/2$: $x = \pi/6$ or $5\pi/6$.

$\sin x = 1$: $x = \pi/2$.

**All:** $\{\pi/6,\ \pi/2,\ 5\pi/6\}$.
</details>

You have just used **principal values**, **general solutions**, and the
**quadratic-in-trig substitution** trick. Now we name them.

::: {.fun-fact}
The general-solution formula
$x = n\pi + (-1)^n\alpha$
hides a small piece of mathematical history. Indian astronomers in the
*Sūrya-Siddhānta* (c. 4th–5th century CE) tabulated planetary
positions as solutions to equations of the shape "find the angle whose
sine equals such-and-such" and listed *all* periodic solutions
explicitly — not just the principal one. The **periodicity** of
trigonometric solutions was understood in working astronomy a thousand
years before Euler turned the same fact into the formula above by
folding the two branches $\alpha$ and $\pi - \alpha$ into a single
$(-1)^n$.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A trig equation has a **family** of solutions, not a single answer,
because the trig functions are periodic. The general-solution formula
encodes both the *principal* angle $\alpha$ and the *periodic copies*
in one line — and every harder equation in the chapter is a routine
reduction to one of these three families.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

The **general solutions** of the basic equations:

$$\sin x = \sin\alpha \iff x = n\pi + (-1)^n \alpha,\quad n \in \mathbb{Z}.$$

$$\cos x = \cos\alpha \iff x = 2n\pi \pm \alpha,\quad n \in \mathbb{Z}.$$

$$\tan x = \tan\alpha \iff x = n\pi + \alpha,\quad n \in \mathbb{Z}.$$

**Reduction strategy** for any trigonometric equation:

1. Use identities to express everything in one trig function.
2. Reduce to one of the three basic forms.
3. Apply the matching general-solution formula.
4. Trim to the requested interval if asked.

:::

A short verse:

> *Sine repeats with a twist of $(-1)^n$;*
> *Cosine swings $\pm$ around two-pi-n.*
> *Tangent is simpler — one pattern per pi —*
> *The chapter is three formulas. Memorise them. That's why.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve $2\cos^2 x + 3\sin x = 0$ on $[0, 2\pi)$.

How a competent solver thinks:

1. Convert everything to $\sin x$ using $\cos^2 x = 1 - \sin^2 x$:
   $2(1 - \sin^2 x) + 3\sin x = 0$.
2. Rearrange: $2\sin^2 x - 3\sin x - 2 = 0$.
3. Quadratic in $u = \sin x$: $(2u + 1)(u - 2) = 0$, so $u = -1/2$ or
   $u = 2$.
4. $\sin x = 2$: impossible.
5. $\sin x = -1/2$: $x$ in Q III or Q IV. Reference angle $\pi/6$.
   $x = \pi + \pi/6 = 7\pi/6$, or $x = 2\pi - \pi/6 = 11\pi/6$.

**Answer:** $x \in \{7\pi/6,\ 11\pi/6\}$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** General solution of $\cos x = -1/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos x = \cos(2\pi/3)$, so $x = 2n\pi \pm 2\pi/3$.
</details>

**W2.** General solution of $\tan x = -1$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan x = \tan(-\pi/4)$, so $x = n\pi - \pi/4$.
</details>

**W3.** Solve $\sin 2x = 0$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin 2x = 0 \Rightarrow 2x = n\pi$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x = n\pi/2$. In $[0, 2\pi)$: $\{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $\cos 2x + 3\cos x + 2 = 0$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\cos 2x = 2\cos^2 x - 1$. Substitute and quadratic-solve.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\cos^2 x - 1 + 3\cos x + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow 2\cos^2 x + 3\cos x + 1 = 0$.

Let $u = \cos x$: $(2u + 1)(u + 1) = 0$, so $u = -1/2$ or $u = -1$.

$\cos x = -1/2$: $x = 2\pi/3$ or $4\pi/3$.

$\cos x = -1$: $x = \pi$.

**All:** $\{2\pi/3,\ \pi,\ 4\pi/3\}$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $\tan x + \cot x = 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\cot x = 1/\tan x$. Multiply through by $\tan x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\tan^2 x + 1 = 2\tan x \Rightarrow (\tan x - 1)^2 = 0 \Rightarrow
\tan x = 1$.

General solution: $x = n\pi + \pi/4$.
</details>

**P3.** Solve $\sin x + \sin 3x = 0$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum-to-product: $\sin x + \sin 3x = 2\sin 2x \cos x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\sin 2x \cos x = 0 \Rightarrow \sin 2x = 0$ or $\cos x = 0$.

$\sin 2x = 0$: $x = 0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$.

$\cos x = 0$: $x = \pi/2, 3\pi/2$ (already in the list).

**All:** $\{0,\ \pi/2,\ \pi,\ 3\pi/2\}$.
</details>

**P4.** Find the general solution of
$\tan 2x = -\cot\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Equations mixing $\tan$ and $\cot$ are unfriendly. Convert the $\cot$
to $\tan$ first using the allied-angle rule $-\cot\alpha =
\tan(\alpha + \pi/2)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Once both sides are $\tan$, apply $\tan x = \tan y \Rightarrow
x = n\pi + y$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Use $-\cot\alpha = \tan(\alpha + \pi/2)$ with $\alpha = x + \pi/3$:

$$-\cot\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3}\right) = \tan\!\left(x + \dfrac{\pi}{3} + \dfrac{\pi}{2}\right) = \tan\!\left(x + \dfrac{5\pi}{6}\right).$$

So the equation becomes $\tan 2x = \tan(x + 5\pi/6)$, hence

$$2x = n\pi + x + \dfrac{5\pi}{6} \;\Rightarrow\; x = n\pi + \dfrac{5\pi}{6},\quad n \in \mathbb{Z}.$$

The trick — converting all functions to one type *before* applying a
general-solution formula — is the engine of Section 3.5.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Solve $\sin x + \cos x = 1$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide by $\sqrt{2}$ and use $\sin(x + \pi/4) = \sin x \cos(\pi/4) + \cos x \sin(\pi/4)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sin x + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\cos x = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$,

i.e. $\sin(x + \pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$.

So $x + \pi/4 = n\pi + (-1)^n \pi/4$. Try $n = 0$: $x = 0$. Try $n = 1$:
$x + \pi/4 = \pi - \pi/4$, so $x = \pi/2$.

In $[0, 2\pi)$: $\{0,\ \pi/2\}$.
</details>

**S2.** Solve $\cos 3x + \cos 2x = \sin\dfrac{3x}{2} + \sin\dfrac{x}{2}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sum-to-product on each side. Then equate.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS: $\cos 3x + \cos 2x = 2 \cos\dfrac{5x}{2} \cos\dfrac{x}{2}$.

RHS: $\sin\dfrac{3x}{2} + \sin\dfrac{x}{2} = 2\sin x \cos\dfrac{x}{2}$.

Equation: $2\cos(x/2)[\cos(5x/2) - \sin x] = 0$.

Case A: $\cos(x/2) = 0 \Rightarrow x/2 = \pi/2 + n\pi \Rightarrow x = (2n+1)\pi$.

Case B: $\cos(5x/2) = \sin x = \cos(\pi/2 - x)$.
$5x/2 = 2k\pi \pm (\pi/2 - x)$.

Plus sign: $5x/2 + x = 2k\pi + \pi/2 \Rightarrow 7x/2 = 2k\pi + \pi/2
\Rightarrow x = (4k+1)\pi/7$.

Minus sign: $5x/2 - x = 2k\pi - \pi/2 \Rightarrow 3x/2 = 2k\pi - \pi/2
\Rightarrow x = (4k-1)\pi/3$.

(Combine and de-duplicate as needed.)
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark (objective).** General solution of $\sin x = -1$ is
(a) $x = n\pi$ (b) $x = (2n+1)\pi/2$ (c) $x = 2n\pi - \pi/2$ (d) $x = n\pi + \pi/2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\sin x = -1$ at $x = -\pi/2$ plus full periods. So
$x = 2n\pi - \pi/2$. **Answer: (c).**
</details>

**2-mark (short answer).** Find general solution of $\cos 2x = 0$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x = (2n+1)\pi/2 \Rightarrow x = (2n+1)\pi/4$.
</details>

**3-mark (short answer II).** Solve $\sin x + \sin 5x = \sin 3x$ on
$[0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin x + \sin 5x = 2\sin 3x \cos 2x$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2 \sin 3x \cos 2x = \sin 3x$, i.e. $\sin 3x (2\cos 2x - 1) = 0$.

Case A: $\sin 3x = 0 \Rightarrow 3x = n\pi \Rightarrow x \in \{0, \pi/3, 2\pi/3, \pi, 4\pi/3, 5\pi/3\}$ on $[0, 2\pi)$.

Case B: $\cos 2x = 1/2 \Rightarrow 2x = \pm\pi/3 + 2n\pi \Rightarrow
x \in \{\pi/6, 5\pi/6, 7\pi/6, 11\pi/6\}$ on $[0, 2\pi)$.

**All ten values:** $\{0, \pi/6, \pi/3, 2\pi/3, 5\pi/6, \pi, 7\pi/6, 4\pi/3, 5\pi/3, 11\pi/6\}$.
</details>

**5-mark (long answer).** (i) Find the general solution of
$\sqrt{3}\cos x + \sin x = 1$.
(ii) List all solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Divide by $2$ and recognise $\cos(x - \pi/6)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos x + \dfrac{1}{2}\sin x = \dfrac{1}{2}$,

i.e. $\cos(x - \pi/3) = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (using $\cos\pi/3 = 1/2$,
$\sin\pi/3 = \sqrt{3}/2$).

So $x - \pi/3 = 2n\pi \pm \pi/3$, giving

$x = 2n\pi$ or $x = 2n\pi + 2\pi/3$.

**(ii)** In $[0, 2\pi)$: $\{0,\ 2\pi/3\}$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Exemplar Ch 3 LA "$\cos 3x + \cos 2x = …$"
mirrors Stretch S2.
**JEE pointer:** the linear-combination trick (Stretch S1, 5-mark) is
JEE Main standard — practice rewriting $a\cos x + b\sin x = c$.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **Every [trigonometric equation](/glossary.qmd#trigonometric-equation) has a [general solution](/glossary.qmd#general-solution-trig)** — an infinite family parametrised by $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ — because [sine, cosine and tangent](/glossary.qmd#trigonometric-function) are periodic.
2. **The strategy is always the same**: use identities to push everything into one trig function, reduce to one of the three basic forms ($\sin x = \sin\alpha$, $\cos x = \cos\alpha$, $\tan x = \tan\alpha$), then write the matching general solution.
3. **Squaring or dividing can corrupt your solution set** — squaring may add extraneous roots, and dividing by $\sin x$ silently throws away the cases where $\sin x = 0$. Always check.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$\sin x = \sin y \Rightarrow x = y$."** No. The general
   solution has the $(-1)^n$ term precisely because *both* $\alpha$ and
   $\pi - \alpha$ have the same sine.
2. **"Squaring is harmless."** Squaring an equation can introduce
   extraneous roots. Always check candidates against the original.
3. **"I can divide by $\sin x$ freely."** Only if $\sin x \ne 0$. The
   safer move is to **factor**, never divide. Concrete example: for
   $\sin 2x + \cos x = 0$, expanding $\sin 2x = 2\sin x\cos x$ gives
   $\cos x(2\sin x + 1) = 0$. *Dividing by $\cos x$* would silently
   throw away the family $\cos x = 0$ (i.e. $x = (2n+1)\pi/2$).
   Factoring keeps both branches; the union is the general solution.
4. **"Mixed $\tan$ and $\cot$ in one equation can't be combined."**
   Use the allied-angle rule $-\cot\alpha = \tan(\alpha + \pi/2)$ (or
   $\tan\alpha\cdot\cot\alpha = 1$ if signs agree) to push everything
   into a single function. See Working P4.
5. **"Just pick the principal value and you're done."** The board
   marks for *general solution* answers require the $n$-parameter
   family. Stopping at the principal value loses marks even if the
   principal is correct.
:::

### Self-Assessment

Be honest with yourself.

1. Could you write the three general-solution formulas from memory?
2. Did you solve at least 5 of the 7 *Discover* problems and 5 of the 7
   practice problems without peeking past Hint 1?
3. Can you spot when an equation needs sum-to-product vs a quadratic
   substitution?

- **3 yes:** move on to **[Complex Numbers](../04-complex-numbers/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo Stretch S2 and the 5-mark item.
- **0–1 yes:** redo *Discover*. Equations are won by pattern recognition
  — drill the canonical reductions until they're automatic.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[Compound Angle Identities](../03b-trig-identities-compound-angles/index.qmd)** —
  sum-to-product and double-angle formulas reduce many equations to the
  basic three.

### What this unlocks

- **[Class 12 — Continuity & Differentiability](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/04-continuity/index.qmd)** —
  zeros of trig functions reappear when finding critical points.
- **[Inverse Trigonometric Functions](../../tier-cbse-12-class-12/02-inverse-trigonometric-functions/index.qmd)**.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 11, Chapter 3 — Trigonometric Functions** (NCERT 2023
  rationalised). Section 3.5: trigonometric equations and their general
  solutions.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:**
  [`t3.solving-trig-equations`](../../tier-3-algebra2-geometry-trig/solving-trig-equations/index.qmd).

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Game development.** Aiming an arrow at a moving target requires
solving $\sin\theta = \dfrac{v_y}{v}$ for the launch angle. Two
solutions: a low arc and a high arc. The general-solution formula tells
you both at once.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research.** Eigenvalues of rotation matrices land on the unit circle.
Finding eigenvalues of structured matrices reduces to trigonometric
equations — a setting where the pattern $(-1)^n$ explains why
discrete-spectrum systems repeat.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

Touchstones: Russian school (Discover problems force the three families
to crystallise), Bhāskara (verse summarises the three formulas),
Better Explained ("the equation has a *family* of answers" framing).
:::
